serving.natural_language_portion: A typical small side serving of ajvar as a condiment or dip is approximately 30 g (about 2 tablespoons), providing around 36 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Ajvar, roasted red pepper and eggplant relish (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods closest match: sweet red pepper, roasted, with oil)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for ajvar. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for roasted red peppers (FDC ID 170108), eggplant cooked (FDC ID 169228), and vegetable oil (FDC ID 172336), weighted to a typical ajvar formulation (approx. 60% red pepper, 20% eggplant, 15% vegetable oil, 5% seasoning). Input record values (energy 120 kcal, protein 1.5 g, fat 9.0 g, carbohydrate 8.0 g, sodium 250 mg, fibre 3.0 g, sugar 4.0 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained as the best available estimate. Calcium 15 mg, iron 0.5 mg, vitamin C 30 mg, cholesterol 0 mg, saturated fat 1.0 g, trans fat 0.0 g are also consistent with USDA component data and are retained.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on roasted red pepper / mixed vegetable relish category.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ajvar in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with roasted red peppers and mixed vegetable condiments, which consistently fall in the 10–20 GI range due to high water content, fibre (3 g/100 g), and low net carbohydrate (5 g/100 g after fibre). Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (15 × 5 g net carb per 100 g × 0.30) / 100 ≈ 0.4. This is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with peer-reviewed vegetable GI data.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 250 mg per 100 g; borderline for a condiment consumed in small portions
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit ajvar to small portions (≤30 g per serving) and choose low-sodium or home-prepared versions, as sodium at 250 mg/100 g can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets (≤2000 mg/day per WHO) when consumed in larger quantities; the potassium and fibre from red peppers and eggplant provide partial DASH-diet offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (5 g/100 g after fibre), high fibre (3 g/100 g), low GI (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ajvar is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in typical condiment portions (30 g), as its low GI (~15), high dietary fibre (3 g/100 g), and low net carbohydrate content (approximately 5 g/100 g) result in a negligible glycemic load (~0.4 per serving); however, the oil content (9 g fat/100 g) adds caloric density and should be factored into total energy management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high vitamin C (30 mg/100 g), antioxidant-rich red pepper base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ajvar presents a low cardiovascular risk profile in condiment-sized portions due to negligible saturated fat (1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and a high antioxidant load from roasted red peppers (vitamin C 30 mg/100 g); sodium at 250 mg/100 g warrants portion control in individuals with established cardiovascular disease or heart failure where sodium restriction is clinically indicated.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (red peppers, eggplant) contain solanine alkaloids that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor personal response to ajvar, as its primary ingredients — red peppers and eggplant — are nightshade vegetables containing solanine alkaloids that can trigger or worsen joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; solanine sensitivity varies per person and some RA patients tolerate nightshades without issue.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ajvar is a traditional Balkan roasted red pepper and eggplant relish originating from the Western Balkans, particularly Serbia, North Macedonia, Croatia, and Bosnia. It is made by roasting red bell peppers and eggplant until charred, then peeling, blending, and cooking them down with vegetable oil, garlic, salt, and vinegar to produce a thick, spreadable condiment. The texture is smooth to slightly chunky depending on preparation style, and the flavour profile is rich, smoky, mildly sweet from the peppers, and subtly tangy from the vinegar. Ajvar is served as a condiment, dip, or spread alongside grilled meats, flatbreads, and vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 120 kcal, 1.5 g protein, 9.0 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from vegetable oil), 8.0 g carbohydrate, 3.0 g dietary fibre, 4.0 g sugar, and 250 mg sodium. It is naturally cholesterol-free, gluten-free, vegan, and halal. The high vitamin C content (30 mg/100 g) from red peppers supports immune function and iron absorption. Its low glycemic index (~15) and high fibre make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content warrants portion awareness for hypertension management. Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should note the nightshade vegetable base. Ajvar is widely available commercially in jars and is also prepared at home in large batches during autumn pepper harvest season across the Balkans.
common_user_queries: Can I eat ajvar with rheumatoid arthritis?
Is ajvar the same as roasted red pepper dip?

confidence_reason: No exact ajvar entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central component data for roasted red peppers, cooked eggplant, and vegetable oil, weighted to a typical ajvar formulation. The input record values are consistent with this composite and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed vegetable GI literature. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for ajvar as a composite dish is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2024; the fat is predominantly unsaturated (sunflower or canola oil base) but exact MUFA/PUFA split is not available without a specific product analysis. potassium_mg is set to -1: while red peppers and eggplant are potassium-rich, a composite ajvar value is not reported in any approved source; estimated ~280 mg/100 g from component data but not confirmed. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for composite ajvar preparation not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a plant-oil-based relish with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with USDA component data. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as ajvar contains no animal-derived ingredients. The input record incorrectly listed primary_cuisine as 'Middle Eastern' and country_of_origin as 'Singapore'; ajvar is a Balkan dish originating from Serbia and North Macedonia — corrected in this output. Cultural tags (Shawarma, Kebab) from the input are not applicable to ajvar and have been removed. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not directly support the nutrient values and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved adapted source.
